does: This paper proposes Follow-up Likelihood as Reward (FLR), a novel reward mechanism that uses the probability of a user's next utterance as a signal to evaluate language model responses. Rather than relying on human annotations or commercial LLM judges, FLR infers whether a response was appropriate by measuring how naturally the user's follow-up utterance flows from it. The authors demonstrate that FLR matches or exceeds the performance of reward models trained on large-scale human-annotated and GPT-4-annotated preference data across eight pairwise-preference benchmarks and four rating-based benchmarks. They further propose automatically mining preference pairs from a base policy model's generations using FLR, then using these pairs to fine-tune the model and improve its helpfulness through alignment without external annotation.
connects: This work builds on the long line of research into reward modeling and RLHF for language model alignment, which has relied on expensive human preference annotations or proxy signals like GPT-4 judgments. It extends natural language understanding approaches that use contextual likelihood for downstream tasks, and connects to broader work on learning from implicit user feedback signals in dialogue systems and human-computer interaction. The core insight parallels conversation analysis research showing that next-turn behavior reveals participant satisfaction, while also relating to recent work exploring self-improvement mechanisms where models generate their own training data without external feedback. It responds to the practical challenge of scaling alignment to domains where obtaining high-quality preference annotations is costly or infeasible.
enables: This work opens pathways toward fully autonomous alignment methods that require no human preference data or commercial model access, making alignment more accessible and scalable for diverse applications and languages. The approach could extend to other sequential prediction tasks where follow-up behavior provides natural feedback signals. It enables research into weaker, more efficient reward models and potentially cheaper fine-tuning pipelines. Future work could explore combining FLR with other implicit feedback signals, applying it to multimodal settings where user reactions include visual feedback, or using it for continuous online learning where models improve from live user interactions. The method also raises questions about gaming follow-up likelihood and robustness to distribution shift, pointing toward research in adversarial evaluation and more sophisticated implicit feedback models.
